does: This paper examines the institutional question of who should conduct evaluations of advanced AI systems by analyzing nine historical regulatory evaluation regimes across industries. It develops a framework for determining the appropriate role distribution between public bodies (AISIs and governments) and private evaluators for different types of advanced AI assessments. The paper recommends that public institutions directly conduct safety-critical evaluations, particularly those requiring gray- and white-box access to AI models, while identifying which evaluation categories might be effectively delegated to private entities. It also estimates the technical and financial capacity requirements for public bodies to perform rigorous advanced AI evaluation.
connects: This work responds to the emerging governance challenge created by rapid advanced AI development and builds on prior research in technology regulation and risk assessment frameworks. It draws parallels to established evaluation regimes in pharmaceuticals, aviation, financial services, and other high-stakes industries where public and private evaluators have developed complementary roles. The paper engages with existing AI governance discussions about appropriate regulatory structures and extends economic analyses of public versus private evaluation capacity. It addresses tensions identified in prior work on AI safety and transparency—particularly around information sensitivity, verification costs, and conflict-of-interest dynamics—and applies regulatory economics principles to the novel context of large language models and advanced AI systems.
enables: This framework could inform how AI Safety Institutes structure their operations and determine resource allocation priorities as they become operational worldwide. The paper's capacity estimates could guide government budgeting for AI evaluation infrastructure and help policymakers understand the realistic scope of public evaluation under different institutional models. It establishes grounds for international coordination on evaluation standards and public-private division of labor, potentially supporting harmonized approaches across jurisdictions. The work also enables more systematic thinking about what evaluation capabilities governments must build internally versus what can be outsourced, informing debates about whether private evaluation ecosystems can meaningfully reduce public burden or whether safety-critical assessment inevitably requires deep public technical capacity.
